title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of real operational cost data for parks. Trustee Barb supported the increase citing population growth and fairness. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this creates a regional inspection sticker system where Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Cloverland, and Riverside will mutually recognize fire inspections for food trucks. One inspection sticker from any participating municipality will be valid across all communities. Motion by Lope Sorrell, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to Section 1.08 regarding virtual meeting attendance. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the written policy still contained language about health, safety, and welfare justifications that contradicted the previous meeting's vote to make attendance more broad. After discussion, the board voted to strike sentences requiring specific reasons for remote attendance, keeping only requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and prohibition from closed sessions. Amendment passed 5-2 with Hermling and Motti voting no. The original motion as amended passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, passed unanimously. Parks, HR/Finance, and one CLIPS position remain open. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street requested licenses. Chief Hunt explained the license requirement protects against stolen merchandise sales and involves posting items on a police-accessible website. Since the building is still under construction, the board amended the motion to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Amendment and original motion both passed unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Concerns included parking on grass areas along Alderson Street and safety issues. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots. Trustee Beck questioned costs but was assured the market operates at no loss through vendor fees and grant funding for EBT/token programs. Mont Miller Park was considered but rejected as too remote. No action was taken; discussion only.

Public Works Director Michael reported conducting speed studies showing the 85th percentile speed at 45 mph. The road was under construction preventing completion of the study. He indicated findings would be brought back to the board. A resident had complained the speed limit remains inconsistent with the ordinance over two months after the board directed a change.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the board for hiring outside engineering consultants for $108,000 while an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer board meeting before being officially appointed, calling it inappropriate government action outside the boardroom. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet and called for greater administrator accountability.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased to $761 single family, $563 duplex, $495 multifamily effective upon ordinance passage
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm as joint meeting with Plan Commission
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Weston Avenue speed limit study to be completed and brought back to board
Applicant resumes for committee appointments to be included in future board packets
Scoffield Coin and Hobby license contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Remote meeting attendance policy updated - stricter language removed
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee

title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to develop $100/day fines for illegal sanitary sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects and acknowledged positive feedback on the Kennedy Park Farmers Market setup.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue settlement repairs by Integrity Grading, Scoffield Avenue concrete and manhole repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration work.

Staff reported on spring road maintenance including removing road limits April 29th, mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and shoulder work. Significant dewatering efforts were required along the Weston Avenue tributary due to high water levels threatening Crane Meadows area. Staff discussed potential permanent pump solutions. Spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick area were completed. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installing a third 18-inch culvert.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and ongoing private well sampling. Sanitary side experienced heavy pump station activity due to clear water infiltration. April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration from sump pump discharges and groundwater intrusion. Staff identified specific violations on Kathleen Street lift station.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total, with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term sustainable funding for state transportation. He noted while grant funding helps one-time costs, recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded, requiring general tax dollar subsidies. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. He demonstrated cost savings showing the village's crushed material costs $17.11/cubic yard versus $28/cubic yard paid by City of Wausau, saving approximately $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Josh and Michael discussed significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary system, largely from illegal sump pump connections. The committee debated fine amounts, with code allowing $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members distinguished between unknowing versus willful violations. Trustee Zagami suggested starting at $100/day, which adds up quickly. The committee directed staff to draft enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction, when they would pay assessment for main extension. Committee added language specifying reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction. Weston's share covers utility relocation at an estimated $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented consultant proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik intersection project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; MSA recommended due to their roundabout design involvement. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 and Clark Dietz $73,730; Clark Dietz recommended due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to recommend MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to draft sanitary sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day
Recommend village board approve Radkkey Avenue temporary utility connection agreement with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Recommend MSA for Alderson/Jelnik intersection construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact for future meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update for future meeting
Norcon to begin Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday of current week

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking a proactive approach to protecting municipal water sources.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive (Item 6 taken first)
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included the optional storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be located further north to allow for future building expansion. The motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling practices. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Commissioner Zagami commended the well-organized program. The motion to approve the program with reports back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She agreed to send email notifications and calendar invites to commissioners.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted as a future agenda item for fall. A commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous concerns about the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure accountability.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department site plan recommended for Board of Trustees approval with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reports to Plan Commission and Public Works
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks wellhead protection conditional use approval
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email notifications and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Ehlers residential TIF presentation to be shared with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, providing educational presentations from department heads including the Health Department, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The only formal action taken was approval of the April 1st meeting minutes, which passed unanimously. No policy items were brought forward for action.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg participating remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items presented
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Discussion of next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff across four teams: environmental health (overseeing 1,200+ licenses), nursing, community health improvement, and administration. Suginaka highlighted the new homeless system response position created through partnership with the City of Wausau, and discussed public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits. Administrator Leonard noted about half of the department's $5 million budget is tax levy, with environmental health services designed to be self-supporting.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent director Christa, explaining the department's nearly 140 FTE staff across divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Of their $22 million revenue budget, approximately $7 million is tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys program addressing housing instability, which was piloted with state funding and substantially modified based on board feedback. The program addresses root causes of housing instability, noting many participants had never had credit accounts or formal leases.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his department increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE this year. In 2025, they had 8,149 interactions with veterans and families, up from approximately 5,900 the prior year. Galindo reported veterans in Marathon County received $2.6 million in retroactive VA payments in 2025, with total annual benefits of $44 million flowing into the county. The department is focusing on surviving spouse benefits, housing stability (partnering with Center for Veterans Issues), and suicide prevention (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). Supervisor Kremes asked about staffing adequacy, and Galindo acknowledged they don't have enough time but do well with current resources.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) with about 60 FTE staff and 385-400 volunteers. The organization receives about 11% of its budget from county levy, with Marathon County contributing $413,135. Ray described significant financial challenges, noting federal Older Americans Act funding has remained flat while nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years. The organization has had to reduce meals served, service days, and staffing. Ray noted they hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate nutrition program at the Hmong American Center.

Chair Boots explained that the county completed its comprehensive plan last session and will be working on a new strategic plan, targeting completion by December. He indicated he will visit all standing committee meetings to discuss how each committee will follow through on strategic plan objectives, noting that educational presentations should advance strategic plan goals.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report data to full board and potentially return for more extensive committee presentation
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested agenda items for future meetings

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris related to the recent tornado and flooding disaster affecting Ringle and other communities. Two affected residents spoke requesting the county waive all fees, but the board approved the Solid Waste Management Board's recommendation to reduce fees to $36.94 per ton (covering costs) rather than eliminating them entirely.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation and remarks by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. Gibbs noted 34% of the board is in its first or second term. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large Executive Committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous with no discussion.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fees Modification for Disaster Debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle waived its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (covering costs only). Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive all fees entirely for affected residents. Supervisor Rosenberg, Supervisor Gao, Supervisor Leur, Supervisor Robinson, and others asked questions about timelines, insurance coverage, and policy implications. Administrator Leonard mentioned exploring a partnership with the Community Foundation to help cover unreimbursed costs for landowners. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board. Motion on the resolution by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash. The resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: No formal public comment period was held, but Supervisor Rosenberg requested special dispensation for two residents to speak on the landfill tipping fees resolution. Jessica Toasty spoke for three minutes, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving fees and asking Marathon County to do the same, stating one business has incurred $40,000 in hauling expenses and that residents are paying deductibles for items not covered by insurance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, noting some of the 44 affected homes are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requesting more time for fee waivers.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Landfill tipping fees reduced to $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on mechanism to reimburse unreimbursed debris disposal costs for landowners
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Solid Waste Management Board may revisit timeline extension at future meetings if needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that would only waive reconnect fees for septic systems for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received department overviews from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Introduction to departments appearing before committee
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning land from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; staff explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff's recommendation to waive county zoning and septic system fees for tornado-damaged properties, noting the governor's emergency declaration. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to other disaster victims not in declared areas. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved on a non-unanimous vote to forward to the county board.

David Hagenbusher presented on Solid Waste operations, covering the landfill's history, tipping fees, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032. Tom Lovelin presented on Parks, Recreation and Forestry, noting the department serves both county and city, operates 17 county parks and 30,700 acres of county forest. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after 35 years of service. Lori Mskimmons began the Conservation Planning and Zoning presentation before the transcript ended.

actionItems: Rezoning petition for Tim Veland/James Offer/David Tract approved and forwarded to County Board
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz approved and forwarded to County Board
Amended fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) forwarded to County Board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, recommending approval of amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight winter parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Proposed e-bike ordinance amendments to Chapter 82
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt raised concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking. He questioned whether village code allows parking on grass and noted ADA-compliant handicap spaces should be on firm surfaces rather than grass. He also questioned the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks and expressed concern about long-standing code violations not being resolved.

Roman/Audrey reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day). The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet) that were switched in the text per previous meeting discussion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. The committee discussed whether to exempt children's play scooters but decided to allow officer discretion rather than codifying exemptions. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with emotos prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM - 4 PM, October 31 - May 1) far exceeds surrounding municipalities which typically have 5-hour windows around 2-7 AM. Michael from BPW noted plowing doesn't begin until around 4 AM and residential streets aren't reached until 7-8 AM. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight restrictions, preferring uniform rules to clear roads before plowing. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify 6-8 problem areas for potential targeted signage and bring back recommendations in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest for parishioners crossing Alderson Street. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost around $2,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-$30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for private entity requests. Motion by Olsen, seconded by John, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive covers the installation cost passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program where Weston, Stevens Point, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Clover will mutually recognize food truck inspections with a sticker system. One recent inspection failed due to improper galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane containers. City of Wausau opted out preferring their own system. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having nine separate addresses for one structure requiring review. Motion passed unanimously to recommend corrections to the Board of Trustees.

Staff requested authorization for a raze order on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011 that has been posted since 2021. The fire department previously declined a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Staff noted chickens had been housed in the structure. Current owners prefer this approach with costs added to their tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, expressing concern about grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether current village code allows parking on grass. He raised concerns about ADA-compliant handicap parking being placed on grass rather than firm asphalt surfaces, questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases suggesting better accountability reporting is needed.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael (BPW) to identify 6-8 problem areas for targeted winter parking signage and return in July
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Correct addresses at 5915 Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue properties, and 2211 Radkey Avenue
Investigate Weston Villa Apartments addressing with nine addresses for one structure
Authorize raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Village of Weston Tourism Commission was scheduled to elect new officers and consider a $10,000 grant request for the Taste N Glow Balloon Fest. The meeting was set to address leadership positions for the commission and evaluate funding for a major annual event expected to draw 15,000 attendees to the area.

agenda.item: Election of Officers: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary
Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 21, 2026
Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest

discussions.body: The commission was scheduled to elect leadership positions including Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary. Current commission members eligible for these positions include Ermeling, Frederick, Hodell, Jalowitz, and Zeyghami.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest
discussions.body: The commission was set to consider a $10,000 room tax grant request from Taste N Glow Balloon Fest for their annual two-day event scheduled for July 10-11, 2026 in Marathon City. The event expects approximately 15,000 attendees and 45 estimated room nights, with funds to be used for advertising, potential shuttles, and lodging expenses for balloon pilots and crew members.

publicComment: Public comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on election of Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary
Expected to consider approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Scheduled to vote on $10,000 grant request for Taste N Glow Balloon Fest

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Finance & Human Resource Committee was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026 to review April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details, as well as receive a presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This routine monthly meeting was set to provide fiscal oversight of village finances and departmental operations.

agenda.item: Approval of Minutes from Previous Finance & HR Meeting: April 21, 2026
TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget

discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to acknowledge the April 2026 financial report for all funds. According to the attached report, general fund revenue stood at 70.3% of budget while expenditures were at 40.7% of budget, with staff monitoring several line items that may exceed their budgets.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the monthly Tax Increment Financing district reports for TIF 1 and TIF 2. These reports provide ongoing monitoring of the village's tax increment financing districts.
The committee was expected to acknowledge the legal details report for April, providing oversight of the village's legal expenditures.

discussions.item: Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to receive an educational presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This follows a similar presentation format from the previous month when Planning & Development operations were reviewed.

publicComment: Public comments section was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to acknowledge April Budget Status Report
Expected to acknowledge TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Expected to acknowledge Legal Details for April

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Expected to consider action regarding graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Expected to consider action on Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

The committee considered the minutes from the April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee meeting. The outcome of any approval vote is not recorded in the official vote records.

The committee received a discussion item providing an overview of the Community Development Department. This was an informational item with no action required.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects

Mayoral appointments confirmed to Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board
Amendments to Ordinance 02-0432 as stated by Alder Watson and Alder Tierney to be reviewed by City Attorney
